title: Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23822 ($100,763,014,046) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23822 ($306,277,104,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23822. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades and Extraction Workers
First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $260,272 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238220); class <5: 63,588 firms, avg revenue/firm $386,166 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,739 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,175 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,348,756 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12,459 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,960,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,643 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9,415 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,892,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $787,462 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,152 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,865,618 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,969,303 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 215 firms, avg revenue/firm $311,824,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,821,217 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,152

current_adoption_pct.source: ServiceTitan 2026 State of AI in the Trades
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 12% have already embedded AI in their processes; 34% actively experimenting; survey of 1,032 commercial and residential contractors, 57% HVAC/plumbing/electrical

historical_analogs.source: Field Service Management software adoption data (multiple sources including fieldservicesoftware.io, ServiceTitan market commentary)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Mobile-based work order management rose from 54% (2020) to 72% (2024); prior CRM/FSM adoption wave in HVAC/plumbing estimated ~8 years from sub-10% to ~50% penetration (c. 2016 to 2024 trajectory)

owners_60plus_pct.source: HARDI Voice of Contractor Survey 2024 (October 2024, ~1,100 contractors, 61% company owners); supplemented by ctacquisitions.com Boomer Succession Wave Report 2024-2030
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 55-64 age group = 30% of respondents; 65+ = 23%; together 53% are 'nearing retirement age' (55+). Boomer-owned share of home services segments runs 55-70% in operator-survey samples. Owner age 60+ estimated at 38% (approximating half of 55-64 cohort at 15% + 65+ at 23%).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Exit Planning Institute data: only 35% of HVAC/plumbing/electrical business owners have any formal succession plan (Southern Home Services blog citing EPI, 2024). HARDI survey confirms 53% within 11-20 years of retirement; ACHR News reports 'Gray Tsunami' hitting HVACR industry. Succession shortage is widely documented by ACCA, PHCC, and M&A advisors.

active_consolidators.source: ctacquisitions.com 2026 PE tracker; dealseam.com plumbing PE rollup tracker 2026; pipelineon.com HVAC PE buyer map 2026
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 23+ active PE-backed platforms in plumbing alone plus HVAC overlaps (Apex Service Partners, Sila Services, Wrench Group, Redwood Services, Legacy Service Partners, T3 Services, Pueblo Mechanical, Champions Group, Heartland Home Services, ARS-Rescue Rooter, and others). Apex alone closed ~60 add-on acquisitions in 2025.

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com HVAC Business Valuation Guide 2026; First Page Sage HVAC EBITDA Multiples Report Q1 2025; GF Data Q4 2024
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Tuck-in add-on acquisitions (the buy entry for roll-ups) at 4-8x EBITDA for $1-3M EBITDA firms; main-street under $1M at 3.0-4.5x EBITDA. GF Data Q4 2024 NAICS 238 at $10-25M TEV averaged 5.7x TEV/EBITDA. Median for band-eligible add-on targets in $1-5M EBITDA range estimated at 5.0x EBITDA (EBITDA basis after market-rate owner comp). Note: some sources cite SDE multiples at 2.0-3.0x; conversion to EBITDA basis (adding back market-rate owner comp of ~$150-200K) shifts these to approximately 4-6x EBITDA.

exit_mult.source: PKF O'Connor Davies Summer 2025; ctacquisitions.com HVAC valuation guide; First Page Sage Q1 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: PKF O'Connor Davies Summer 2025: 'transaction multiples have remained elevated (north of 10x EBITDA)' for high-quality $10M+ EBITDA businesses. First Page Sage Q1 2025: residential HVAC $5-10M EBITDA at 9.3-10.8x. Comfort Systems USA acquisition of J&S Mechanical (Feb 2024) at 8.0-10.0x on $12-15M EBITDA. Excluding sponsor-to-sponsor mega-deals (Champions Group ~18.5x, Sila ~17-20x) which represent liquidity events above the typical exit tier. Mid-point of 9-12x range = 10.5x. EBITDA basis verified.

pricing_structure.value: Flat-rate menu pricing dominates residential service/repair (~85-90% of revenue for roll-up target firms); maintenance/service agreements cover ~39% of US HVAC services revenue (Mordor Intelligence 2025/2026); commercial/new-construction work is bid cost-plus-ish (~10-15% of revenue for residential-focused roll-up targets)
pricing_structure.source: ServiceTitan HVAC Pricing Guide 2026; Mordor Intelligence 2025/2026 US HVAC Services Market Report (cited in housecallpro.com 2026 HVAC pricing guide); multiple contractor industry sources
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Many successful HVAC companies use flat rate for 90% of residential work; preventive maintenance contracts captured 39% of total US HVAC services revenue in 2025 (Mordor Intelligence). Maintenance agreements priced at $199-399/year.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Licensed physical trade work — installation, repair, code-compliant pipefitting. AI does not turn wrenches. Small 4% fraction covers job documentation, permit paperwork lookup, and diagnostic assistance tools. EPA licensing and physical presence requirements preclude AI substitution of core tasks.

Physical install and service work requiring EPA 608 refrigerant certification and on-site presence. AI-assisted diagnostics and manuals-on-demand modestly raise wrench-time productivity but do not substitute the technician. 4% reflects documentation and diagnostic-reference tasks only.

Crew coordination and quality control are on-site and cannot be automated. However, scheduling, job costing, progress reporting, and crew dispatch optimization automate substantially with AI tools now available in ServiceTitan and similar platforms. 20% reflects the schedulable/reportable fraction of this role.

The primary AI target in this industry. AI voice agents for call answering/booking are already live (ServiceTitan AI voice agent); invoicing, permit filing, maintenance-agreement renewals, and review management automate near-fully. 80% reflects the highly routine, document/data-centric nature of general office work. Residual 20% for complex exception handling.
Physical fabrication and installation of ductwork and HVAC components. Licensed skilled trade requiring physical presence. Same logic as plumbers: only documentation fraction is automatable.

Takeoffs, bid assembly, commercial estimating, and management reporting are heavily AI-tractable. AI estimating tools (ServiceTitan, PlanSwift, etc.) are displacing manual takeoff. Negotiation with GCs, key-account management, and strategic decisions persist. 25% reflects the estimating/reporting/analytics fraction.
Physical labor and apprentice pipeline. Unaffected by current AI except training-delivery improvements. Minimal automatable fraction.

breakdown_by_role.role: 49-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Same split as construction supervisors: field supervision, quality control and safety oversight remain on-site; scheduling, technician tracking, and parts-consumption reporting automate substantially.
Physical labor assistance. Minimal AI exposure beyond training delivery.

Lead qualification, quote generation, and financing paperwork automate. In-home close of $10-20K replacement jobs stays human under current practice — homeowner psychology requires human trust for major purchases. AI chat/quoting handles the low-complexity service call pipeline. 30% reflects pre-visit and administrative sales tasks.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted roles (dispatch, CSR, bookkeeping, estimators, project managers, permit clerks, apprentices)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The unlisted tail (26.49% of wage bill) contains: (a) dispatchers and CSRs (~10-14% of total wage bill per prompt guidance) — highly automatable via AI voice agents and dispatch optimization; (b) bookkeepers (~3-4%) — automatable via AI accounting tools; (c) permit clerks (~1-2%) — near-fully automatable; (d) estimators/project managers (~3-5%) — partially automatable (AI estimating tools); (e) apprentices and miscellaneous trades (~3-5%) — not automatable. Weighted blended fraction of 35% reflects the mix of highly automatable back-office staff (~60% of this tail at 0.65 fraction) with non-automatable trade tail (~40% at ~0% fraction): 0.60×0.65 + 0.40×0.02 ≈ 0.39+0.008 = 0.40, adjusted down to 0.35 given uncertainty in the composition.

pi_dist.rationale: Residential flat-rate pricing (~85-90% of revenue for roll-up targets) means AI savings on CSR/dispatch/office labor are NOT mechanically passed to customers; the firm captures the margin improvement. However, partial deflation mechanisms exist: (1) PE-scaled national competitors with identical AI tools will eventually re-anchor flat-rate price books downward as the efficiency gain becomes table stakes; (2) lead-gen platforms (Google LSA, Angi/Yelp) own demand generation in many markets and raise per-lead costs, capturing upstream value as contractor costs fall; (3) maintenance agreement pricing is sticky and relationship-controlled but OEM remote monitoring may eventually capture service relationships. Scored 0.55: operator has meaningful but not dominant pricing power; not cost-plus (good), but competitive dynamics and lead-gen platform leakage reduce from the ideal 1.0.
sources: Mordor Intelligence 2025/2026 US HVAC Services Market Report (maintenance agreement share)

pi_moat.rationale: Strong structural moat: (1) state contractor licensing (47 states require licensing for HVAC, plumbing work) legally reserves paid demand through licensed firms; (2) EPA Section 608 certification required for refrigerant handling — cannot be bypassed by AI; (3) equipment warranties typically require OEM-certified dealer installation; (4) physical presence required — AI cannot perform the core service; (5) maintenance agreements create recurring customer relationships. However, moat is not impenetrable: (a) national PE platforms with AI tools and brand recognition compete aggressively for local market share; (b) Google LSA and Angi own discovery/demand generation, reducing local brand moat; (c) OEM remote monitoring programs could disintermediate the service relationship over 10-20 years; (d) some states have lighter licensing regimes. Scored 0.60: strong regulatory and physical barriers, partially offset by platform competition and lead-gen disintermediation.

t50_years.derivation: Current embedded AI adoption = 12% (ServiceTitan 2026 AI in the Trades, N=1,032 contractors). Target = 50%. Gap = 38 percentage points. Historical analog: field service management/CRM adoption in HVAC/plumbing — mobile work order management went from ~54% (2020) to ~72% (2024), roughly +18pp in 4 years; prior CRM/FSM wave c.2016-2024 took approximately 8 years to go from sub-10% to ~50% penetration in this industry. AI faces similar barriers (44% of contractors cite training complexity, 44% cite integration complexity per ServiceTitan survey) but also faster competitive pressure from PE-scaled competitors. Derivation: 38pp gap ÷ ~5pp/year analog pace ≈ 7.6 years; round to 8.0 years (2026→2034). Plausible range: 5-12 years depending on whether lead field-service platforms (ServiceTitan) successfully embed AI as default or contractors remain fragmented.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 6.18pp. A = 1.25 (< 5), so the cross-check 1 tension condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does not apply. A slow adoption pace (t50=8 years) is internally consistent with a moderate but real AI value story: the office/dispatch layer is genuinely automatable but small, and trade labor (~60% of wage bill) is structurally non-automatable.

terminal_value.justification: The core product is a licensed human technician physically installing or repairing mechanical equipment in a building — AI cannot perform this act, and state contractor licensing, EPA Section 608 refrigerant certification, and equipment warranties legally and practically reserve this demand. AI is purely a cost-cutter for the ~17% back-office/estimating layer, not a product replacer. Terminal risks are demand-shape (heat-pump electrification, OEM remote monitoring, smart-equipment self-diagnostics) but none shrinks aggregate demand for licensed on-site service — electrification is a tailwind (more complex equipment, refrigerant transitions). The ~60%+ trade-labor share of the wage bill structurally caps AI's fraction of cost savings. DURABLE: paid demand for licensed on-site mechanical work persists through mature AI era.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V derived from OEWS 5-digit 23822 wage shares and within-role task fractions based on physical vs. automatable task decomposition. A derived from ServiceTitan 2026 AI in the Trades survey (current adoption = 12% embedded) and FSM/CRM historical adoption analog in this exact industry. No reference to PE deal activity, consolidation volume, or M&A trends used for V or A. Confirmed.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~85-90% flat-rate menu pricing for residential service/repair; ~39% of revenue under maintenance/service agreements (recurring); ~10-15% commercial/new-construction on cost-plus/bid basis. Under flat-rate pricing, AI savings on CSR/dispatch/office labor do NOT flow to clients as lower prices — the operator retains the margin improvement since price is set by the price book, not cost-plus. However, competitive deflation occurs over a longer horizon as PE-scaled competitors with identical AI tools re-anchor local price books. pi_dist = 0.55 reflects this partial protection: better than cost-plus industries, but not immune to competitive margin pressure. pi_moat = 0.60 reflects regulatory gatekeeping (licensing, EPA 608) as the primary barrier, offset by platform competition and lead-gen disintermediation.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5 years A=2.0 vs A=1.25 at t50=8; S moves from ~3.14 to ~3.54 at t50=5. At t50=12, A=0.833 and S~2.87. The 5-12 year range spans S from ~2.87 to ~3.54, a ±12% swing around the central estimate.

The unlisted-tail fraction (0.35) and within-role fractions for the office/dispatch layer drive uncertainty. At ARS=0.13, V=2.04 and S~3.03; at ARS=0.22, V=3.09 and S~3.31. Bounded by the ~60% trade-labor floor which is structurally non-automatable.

Prompt flags that n_band=1,152 (100-499 employee class only) likely understates eligible firms; the 9,415-firm 20-99 class at implied 7.96% EBITDA margin yields $787K EBITDA (out of band), but residential-service shops run 10-20% margins (not the Damodaran 7.96%). If residential margin ~15%, many 20-99 firms are in-band. At n_band=5,000, TD=0.924 and B rises to ~7.47; S would increase from 3.14 to ~3.30. This is a systematic undercount.

M = clamp(4*(exit-buy)/buy, 0, 10). At buy=3.5, M=8.0 (capped effect). At buy=6.5, M=2.46. S ranges from ~2.76 (buy=6.5) to ~3.76 (buy=3.5, M=8.0) if exit held constant. The buy_mult is the most sensitive M input.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous code: 238220 blends residential service/replacement (~55-60% of industry revenue for roll-up-eligible firms), commercial mechanical contracting (~25-30%), and new-construction subcontracting (~15-20%). Scored on the DOMINANT residential+light-commercial service/replacement sub-segment, which is the roll-up target. The dataset's n_band=1,152 (Damodaran 7.96% Engineering/Construction margin) substantially understates the true band-eligible count — residential service shops typically run 10-20% EBITDA margins, which would put the 9,415-firm 20-99 employee class (avg implied rev ~$9.9M × 15% = $1.48M EBITDA) well inside the $1-10M band. True n_band likely 2,000-8,000+; this record uses the provided value per rule 6. The labor_share (0.3508) at ancestor level 23822 marked LOW quality; residential service shops have higher labor share due to lower equipment-revenue mix. The trade-labor floor (~57% of wage bill in physical installation roles) caps AI's addressable fraction structurally: V cannot exceed ~3.5 under plausible within-role fractions, making this a physical-trades anchor. The consolidation wave (25+ active PE platforms) is reflected only in B and M per rule 2.
